original_abstract: Single-pixel imaging techniques enable to capture a scene without a direct line of sight to the object, but high-quality imaging has been proven challenging especially in the presence of noisy environmental illumination. Here we present a single-pixel imaging technique that can achieve high-quality images by acquiring their Fourier spectrum. We use phase-shifting sinusoid structured illumination for the spectrum acquisition. Applying inverse Fourier transform to the obtained spectrum yields the desired image. The proposed technique is capable of capturing a scene without a direct view of it. Thus, it enables a feasible placement of detectors, only if the detectors can collect the light signals from the scene. The technique is also a compressive sampling like approach, so it can reconstruct an image from sub-Nyquist measurements. We experimentally obtain clear images by utilizing a detector not placed in direct view of the imaged scene even with noise introduced by environmental illuminations.
humanized_abstract: Single-pixel imaging is a technique capable of imaging an object without having a direct line of sight (LOS) to the object. However, high quality imaging of the object has proven to be very difficult especially in the presence of ambient light of the environment. In this letter, we demonstrated a novel method for single-pixel imaging where the Fourier spectrum of the object is captured and an inverse Fourier transform was used to obtain the high quality image. We used phase-shifting sinusoid structured illumination for recording the spectrum of the object. We demonstrated a non-LOS imaging of the object using a single-pixel detector. Our method not only enables us to obtain high quality images of the object in ambient lighting environments, but it also achieves a compressive sampling like approach to obtain an image from below sub-Nyquist sampling rates. Finally, we have demonstrated clear images of an object behind obstacles while our camera was not in the LOS of the object even in the presence of ambient light.

Our method not only enables us to obtain high quality images of the object in ambient lighting environments, but it also achieves a compressive sampling like approach to obtain an image from below sub-Nyquist sampling rates.
